era_context: The early 15th century continued with the Ming Dynasty under Yongle Emperor at its height, the Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan territories, the Hussite movement building momentum in Bohemia, and the European Renaissance beginning to flourish. The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding under Pachacuti.

events.title: Battle of Grunwald (Tannenberg) aftermath and Polish-Lithuanian consolidation
events.description: The Battle of Grunwald (1410) was a decisive victory of the Polish-Lithuanian alliance over the Teutonic Knights, fought in July 1410. By 1410, the aftermath was being consolidated: the First Peace of Thorn (February 1411) would follow, but in 1410 itself, the political and military consequences were being negotiated. The victory marked a turning point in Eastern European power dynamics, diminishing Teutonic Knight dominance and strengthening Polish-Lithuanian union.
events.certainty_note: The battle occurred in July 1410; the year is well-established in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's Second Treasure Voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's second major voyage of exploration, which had departed in 1407, returned to China in 1410. The expedition had sailed to Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval power. The return marked the completion of a major diplomatic and exploratory mission.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Return date is well-documented in Ming records; dating is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Council of Pisa aftermath and papal schism continuation
events.description: The Council of Pisa (1409) had attempted to resolve the Great Schism by deposing both papal claimants and electing a new pope (Alexander V). However, by 1410, the schism remained unresolved, with three papal claimants still active: Gregory XII (Rome), Benedict XIII (Avignon), and Alexander V (Pisa). The ecclesiastical crisis continued to destabilize Western Christendom.

sources.name: Council of Pisa records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the schism in 1410 is well-documented in papal and conciliar records.

events.title: Henry V becomes King of England
events.description: Henry V ascended to the English throne on March 20, 1413, following the death of his father Henry IV. However, in 1410, Henry (then Prince of Wales) was already a significant political figure, involved in military campaigns in Wales and beginning to assert influence over English policy. His accession was imminent, and his preparations for the Hundred Years' War were underway.
key_figures: Henry V (Prince of Wales, future King)
events.certainty_note: Henry V did not become king until 1413; this entry reflects his rising prominence in 1410, not a coronation event.

events.title: Inca Empire expansion under Pachacuti
events.description: Pachacuti (Pachacutec Inca Yupanqui), who ruled approximately 1438–1471, was not yet emperor in 1410. However, the Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca was in a period of expansion and consolidation. By 1410, the Inca had established control over much of the central Andes and were beginning the military campaigns that would lead to the empire's greatest territorial extent under Pachacuti.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are approximate and based on later Spanish colonial records and oral traditions. Specific events for 1410 are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Henry V's prominence in 1410 is somewhat speculative; while he was certainly active in Welsh campaigns and court politics, the specific significance of 1410 versus adjacent years is not clearly distinguished in sources. The entry on the papal schism reflects continuation rather than a specific event in 1410; no major development occurred in 1410 that distinguished it from 1409 or 1411. Inca chronology is highly uncertain; the assignment of Viracocha Inca's reign to ~1410–1438 is based on later Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions, and modern scholarship debates these dates significantly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1410 is moderate but uneven. European events (Poland-Lithuania, England, the papacy) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, particularly for the Zheng He voyages. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Grunwald victory led to Polish-Lithuanian consolidation and the Peace of Thorn
Return of the second Zheng He treasure voyage
Council of Pisa's failure to resolve the schism led to continued ecclesiastical crisis
Henry V's rising prominence as Prince led to his accession as King
